title: HER STORY...
content: Catherine was born into a southern Saskatchewan farming community in June of 1923 to a Swedish father and an American mother.
Bengough appears on her birth certificate. Bengough also had the region's grocery store, the hospital, the doctor, an eventual airstrip, and was a centrepiece in the broader community.

But Viceroy is where I consider her starting point on the planet. Viceroy had the  telephone operator for the party line, a fuel retailer, a community hall, a nearby well for our drinking water, the grain elevator, a passenger rail service that would squire her, as a young lady, to Moose Jaw where she became a teller for the Royal Bank of Canada, and a church where mom and dad were married on a very rainy day on her birthday in 1952. What then followed was a four mile drive on muddy roads back to the farmhouse which was suitably decked out for the happy couple.

title: AND ONWARD TO CURRENT TIMES...
content: With dad having passed in 2013, mom continued residing alone in her home. Eventually installing two unobtrusive webcams provided my sister Lois and I a visual link that would support her desire to live independently.

But the day arrived when nursing care was required. Lois came to town to help out. We short listed some prospective properties and made the first appointment. To our great fortune, the marketing lady provided some guidance in what to look for (and ask) in each facility as we sought a place equipped to support folks with memory issues.
We selected a place at the other end of the city. It offered a lovely private room, scheduled activities, a chef with elegant dining room service, designer decor.

To add some interest to her day, mom and I would head out daily for an epic multi-hour drive to nowhere with a highlight of stopping for lunch at a nice restaurant. In later years when mobility was becoming problematic, we would do a drive through and enjoy a sandwich in the car.

On occasion, we would drive out to the community airport where pilots train for their licence. Best perch was parking by the mesh fence that parallels the active runway, roll down the windows and watch the small prop planes do touch and goes. Some bounced; some greased it onto the runway.

On another day as we entered the airport grounds, we saw the pilots getting a Bell 206 ready to go. So we parked by the adjacent mesh fence, and rolled down the windows. A few minutes passed after the pilots closed the doors, then the whine of the turbine spooling up reached our ears, then the incense of kerosene reached our nostrils. Another few minutes passed before take off power was applied and it lifted skyward with our hair blowing sideways through the car. Simply fantastic.

Turns out, our shared interest in aviation has spanned our lifetimes. I began training for my private pilot licence back in January of 1975. A few months later, licence in hand, and with a need to accumulate flying hours to get into the commercial pilot program, mom and I (and Sammy the poodle) would hop in a rental plane and go see stuff. A few times, we flew back to Bengough or Assiniboia to visit grandma and uncle Cliff. Clifford was mom's older brother and carried on farming on land granted to grandpa and his two brothers subsequent to their 1902 immigration from Sweden. I echo his quiet disposition and value his refined dining etiquette.
Anyway, I digress. And then the mathematical intersection of longevity and cash flow collided.

The solution was to relocate mom to a suitable (and more modest) facility back across town near her home. As a gracious lady, not a comment was uttered on her new semi-private arrangement. But we continued the epic car rides until she couldn't manage the transition from wheelchair to the car seat.  So from that day onward, mom's entire experience would be the secured second floor memory ward.

And then a health crisis descended upon the globe. Residents were isolated in their room with tray service for meals, the salon closed, and strict visitation measures were implemented to protect the health and wellbeing of everyone inside. I was allowed in Monday 11am and Thursday 11am. So I seized this opportunity to style mom to my best effort with what I had on hand, photograph the result, and send the best onward. Eventually the facility rules were relaxed where I could visit each and every day at 11am. With seven original stylings required each week, the challenge was on.
A red canvas tote bag from mom's preferred bakery was put into service. Basic kit consisted of a comb, a brush, a gel and a pink two speed travel hairdryer.

So with the basic kit organized, onwards and upwards. Over time, however, the basics list expanded to include a selection of pressed face powders, a black knit scarf, a mousse, a binder clip, a 'structured lapel', and a black velvet elastic-waisted pant.

title: EVOLVING FASHION CONCEPTS FROM A HAPPENSTANCE NOVICE STYLIST...
content: Occasional random photos of mom have been snapped over the years, but the kernel that launched this series is elusive. Perhaps a kind comment from Lois and/or Melissa could have, somehow, motivated me to find affordable and novel ways to up my game. At which point it would be completely appropriate to wonder, even out loud: what game?

The launch 'comfort' collection spans the range of basic blouses, cotton tops and elastic-waisted pants that originated from her own closet. For safekeeping, her finer sweaters and scarves are kept with me.

With grateful thanks to the facility, over time they donated several 'accessible' blouses for her use. The backs were ripped down the middle from yoke to hem. The resulting garment allows even the most frail among us to look presentable with no effort expended. Brilliant. Mix and match; try this and that. Keep a loose tab on what pieces in what combinations made the family film archive; would hate for hindsight to spotlight an exact duplicate.

November 19, 2020 started just like a couple thousand days before: the model in her wheelchair. Except that serendipity had unknowingly colluded with me, on this particular day, to toss a beautiful sweater in the tote bag as I headed out the door.

Anyway, the session was unfolding like usual. As usual. Until a split second decision was required: whether to snap a customary photograph du jour or experiment a bit. I boldly lurched forward and seized the moment, sweater in hand, draping the colours on her chest and wrapping the sleeves up and around her neck like a scarf. Quick and easy. No loitering.

Wow. The concept seemed to work well enough. And the result was an incremental improvement on any previous result. So basically, the risk is worth taking. And worth repeating. Repeating in perpetuity until a habit is formed. From this day forward, layering on colours and textures over her regular duds would be the perfect solution to producing a thoughtful photo. And under it all is a wheelchair. And all the while in a modest long term care semi-private room with a model with physical and conversational constraints.

The first photo of the array is most telling: the model, the floral, studio equipment, proportions. Oh my, photos taken at a great distance show a lot of textured beige vinyl wallpaper. Note to self: less background.

The 'colours' collection is a natural progression in sophistication with the introduction of wool scarves, and a rainbow of contemporary colours by way of brand new pairs of tees, $5 each, and still ‘fresh’ from the factory. In time, when every possible colour combination had been exhausted, the next infusion of colour was furnished by the long sleeves of my best shirts. Silk ties next played a supportive role with a hint of colour around the neck, however, they would eventually be layered to imply a silk blouse or high neck camisole until ultimately erupting as faux couture.

Styling will be simplified by utilizing a single solitary item and spread (err, oh my apologies, I mean drape) it artfully across the landscape. And then by adding a silk scarf at the neck, a wool scarf could subsequently be elevated to the status of a shawl. Hmmm. Could be on to something here.
Staging gets personal with the occasional inclusion of a family photo. Interestingly, the floral, on occasion, has a darker hue. I like it.
Suppose it could have been a cloudy day. Or perhaps the sheers were closed. Anyway, I like it. Turns out I could exaggerate the effect by pivoting the floral on its axes.
Photographing was evolving.

A fun side note regarding those tees, I bought all mediums thinking I’d put them in my own closet the following season. Turns out, I tried keto, lost 60 pounds, and now run the risk of generating substantial flutter in a crosswind. Thank you, keto. And thank you to my brilliant GP for the referral.

The 'colours' collection received a late-season course correction with the introduction of ladies polyester elastic-waisted pants. The pants are dangling out back with the legs draped forward over the shoulders.

The lens appreciates the iridescent qualities of the fabric. The stylist appreciates its natural resistance to wrinkles. Validation and success of this concept meant including other ladies polyester elastic-waisted pants later in the series; look for navy blue, purple, teal, coral, olive drab.

The 'professional' collection advanced with the addition of a single 'couture' piece. It's fully reversible with the opposing side making its own individual bold statement. Until now, it's been a runner on the 1965 Heintzman piano on which we kids practiced intermittently diligently in the basement rumpus room. Mom's 1965 Hammond B3 resided in the living room and provided her musical interest up until her relocation to nursing. Interestingly, her muscle memory to play her favourite scores was intact up through her early 90s.

Styling is a bit more exacting. A dab of gel gets a trial run. A dab sure goes a long way. And it sure does set fast. And to familiarize myself with the basics of dressing a woman: right over left; right over left; right over left.
Staging is a bit more dramatic, perhaps a bit theatrical: dark and moody; lovely. Highly stylized. Perhaps a glint of glamour will emerge.

Photographing is becoming a little more routine. The improving quality of mom's presentation to the lens correlates to my dissolving awkwardness hanging out in her personal space (lots of water under many bridges; enough said).

June 10, 2021 introduces a ladies cravat by means of a silk tie. From the narrow end, it is cinched around her neck and left to hang into her lap, then the broad end brought up to her chin and tucked into the resulting band. A more elegant presentation requires nothing more than just a hint of colour at the neck.

July 5, 2021 and the model is now oriented the other way. The bookcase which holds the floral print was originally positioned in the corner of the room near her foot board. One day, it was repositioned opposite in the room and now adjacent to her head board; most likely a medical team needing some extra space since my last visit.

To this point in time, the photo array radiates pleasantness and simplicity and consistency. But certain photos rose naturally above the rest. Wonder why? Me, too. I will attempt to explain in brief, but personal, commentary. As well, successful experimentation of a fabric or concept would surely re-appear in future photos.

August 23, 2021 is a harbinger of the impending fall collection. This fleece jacket is dangling out back with the sleeves draped forward. While this piece is a staple in many a sporty wardrobe, its luxurious nap is only enhanced by the shadow produced from its natural height. And as a bonus, often times, the reverse side is often a different shade. Look for other fleeces in tomato red, grey, and black.

September 9, 2021 exudes glamour and sophistication, however, it belies the background reality of a series of incidental fumbles and blunders. A single $5 mocha cotton tee, a pressed wool scarf, and the piano runner folded in half. Hair sculpting and pressed powder round out the look. All the stars aligned.
November 10, 2021 commemorates reopening of the salon, and with a new professional stylist. Thank you for this lovely doo. And to the stylists who work in the larger care community, thank you.

Reminds me of a story that dates back to the 1970s. Don't recall if Lois was with us, but mom, dad and I went to Bengough for dinner one evening. While our table hugged the back wall, the big picture window, adjacent to the sidewalk, dominated the opposite wall. As our meal progressed, the town ladies provided a constant stream of high style as they strolled past the window. And with the sum of all town stylists likely totalling just one, they all had pretty much the same hairstyle.
Mom, with this doo, would have fit right in. Lovely.

November 15, 2021 validates a mathematical concept of being able, in theory, to create more distinctive looks of the model, than the model's available dates in which she may be available to sit for a photo. In other words, two photos in one session.

As for modelling, Catherine is truly impressive. In the prime of her late 90s, and happy to be styled and staged for a photo. Day after day with only an occasional exception. Weekdays, weekends, holidays makes no difference. Her modelling job interleafs beautifully in her late morning schedule, just before lunch is served.

The 'halo' collection incorporates premium corporate-grade fabrics, advances a palate of pressed face powders and their application techniques, ironing of all the component pieces, extensive use of the 'structured lapel', accessorized accordingly, and a more expressive coif.

November 25, 2021 introduces worsted wool and other fine fabrics sourced from my long dormant corporate menswear. Slacks, parted out from the suit, are hung out behind the wheelchair with the legs draped forward. A scarf conceals the various gaps.
Christmas Day 2021 and mom is sporting a lovely new bed jacket. Thank you, Christmas Angel.

January 3, 2022 welcomes in a new year and with seasonal soirees still in full swing, a little something with a little festive bling would be nice. This reversible wine bag tissue has copper leaves on one side and reflective silver leaves on the other. Assuming the raw edges are aligned, a single fold then yields both colours in equal measure; a second fold naturally produces more of one colour than the other; layer on the model accordingly to the desired affect.

The final photo is a matriarchal studio masterpiece that will endure in our family archives. If for no other reason than her striking resemblance to her father Fritiof. Her ensemble combines a single $5 orange cotton tee, wine bag tissue, velvet pants dangling out back with the legs draped forward then layered over the 'structured lapel'. The floral print is positioned upside down.

February 17, 2022 contrasts equal opposites of the colour band with the copper palette. The daring sweep over the ear and the whisp over the opposing brow frames a determined look of a 70 year old nana, but foreign, and fashionable. And it's great grandson Blaise's birthday today.

May 31, 2022 proves a bemused smile comes from seeing something bemusing. Double dollop of mousse manually applied to dry hair and smooched forward with both hands working in unison, rayon blouse draped horizontally from one shoulder to the other. All the stars aligned. And it's great granddaughter Dylan's birthday today.

June 6, 2022 marks the inaugural appearance of Asian silk. It is a vintage jacket, very petit, hand beaded & stitched and fully lined, with a fitted bodice and structured sleeves. Tangerine with buttercream lining. Its troublesome nature in handling it precluded broader use, but it offered a luxurious shimmery accent in supporting other looks.

Catherine appears apres ski, perhaps St. Moritz, or wherever international jet setters are skiing this season. I overdid the gel and it set faster than what I thought would happen. Apologies to the morning attendant who had the surprise of a cement hard hair wave. Anyway, all the stars aligned. This long sleeve pullover rayon blouse has proven itself a valued and reliable staple.

June 11, 2022 establishes a new level of professional imagery on which future photos would be compared. The night before, a single bag gifted from a business lady friend contained a few biz lady scarves and long sleeved collarless blouses, and it was presented to me at a friends Friday supper.
The bag was modestly sized and feather light. Turns out, half way down was a black knitted scarf. Love black. It's my favourite colour. All thousand shades.

June 20, 2022 introduces jewellery, finally.  By means of a (magnetic) line, literally, of fancy looking bits. It is long enough for a single strand to nestle in the bosom, or a double strand of varying lengths around the neckline. And its weight is helpful in repressing a persistent wrinkle (should one dare persist following being pressed earlier in the morning).
Other pieces eventually join the collection and provide a beautiful finishing touch to the image.
August 29, 2022 validates the concept that two unique looks can be achieved by reimagining the same two pieces on the same day. In this case, an accessible pullover blouse and cotton scarf.
This concept had a trial run earlier in the series, however, it was unnecessarily complicated with the second styling starting with a clean slate.

The 'couture' collection ascended to symbolically mourn as our Sovereign lay in state. It is quite the irony that just two pieces of a noteworthy fabric and a simple piece of jewellery, if nothing else, is all it takes.

In recent photos up to this point, and all those going forward, the model would have just come from a warm air blowout, scalp massage/mousse application, full scalp brushing, styling & sculpting of the coif, and artful application of the pressed face powders.

title: SPA, HAIR , MAKEUP FOR  THE STATIC CENTENARIAN WOMAN...
content: Early Sunday morning and early Thursday morning is 'spa day'. Yay.
Crankiness is generally in the air upon my arrival: she having been 'manhandled' in the shower chair to get things done.
History tells me the aroma of a freshly brewed cup of coffee will likely brighten things up. It might work. Maybe not.
To get things underway, I lean into her ear and ask: "would you like to get all dolled up for a picture?" She almost always nods to the affirmative. She knows the treat that will soon follow.

Let's wheel over to the window, close to where the power plug is, and we can gaze outside as people walk to and from the main entrance, or better yet, watch cars attempt to park in a narrow laneway. Should that prove dull and uninteresting, there is then a constant flow of service vehicles, ambulances and black Dodge minivans with darkened windows with discrete lettering in a faux gold font. Should that prove dull and uninteresting, there is always a cloud morphing into something else on the distant westerly horizon.
Her coffee is placed upon a cafeteria napkin on the windowsill and it remains within easy reach during the imminent salon treatment.

Whether her hair is still moist from the 'spa' or bone dry on the alternating days, a warm air blow out on low fan feels wonderful. Blow things this way, then that way, then back the first way. Finish off with a full scalp brushing. And don't get the hair dryer cord tangled up with the wheelchair grip. Or worse yet, drag the cord across her head. Sure is lots to consider so not to derail from things heavenly.

And with a theatrical flourish, come around front and approach her with a can of mousse. My finger points to the label which indicates it is for volume and body. She leans in to read the label and nods her acceptance. And with a few gingerly shakes of the canister, turn it upside down and press the button and voila: a mound of whipped cream. Quite marvellous, really. Apply less than half on top and sides with the majority heading around back to tame the bulk. Some of the best styles come about by letting the mousse mostly dry combed one direction, then combing it the other way. Perhaps an extra dollop of mousse is required to tease a wave or perhaps generate some altitude up top. And its lovely aroma would linger. And its lovely aroma would awaken on a subsequent day with warm water. The resulting hairdos are completely random and, try as I may, have proven immune to a repeat.

And without the distraction of an intermission, Act II gets underway with a dab of pressed face powder. Again, I come around front, compact in hand, and present it to her for inspection. She recognizes the brand name and acknowledges that with a slight nod. So with great fanfare, the lid opens, taking care not to fumble the small brush, delicately sweep the brush around the compact all the while chatting about how the chosen shade will go with the day's colour pick, and then gently apply the first stroke on her left cheek. She leans forward a bit to meet the brush half way. Her eyes follow the brush as it returns to the compact. Do a matching appliqué on her right cheek, and then onward for a seamless coverage. Perhaps a darker tone or two for a contour. The final appliqué is the darkest chocolate brown for her eyelids. She deftly knows the routine and briefly closes her eyes.
And then, avoiding a sudden move, will carefully approach with her cleaned glasses and place them with precise tenderness.

title: STYLING TECHNIQUES FOR THE ACTIVE-APPEARING CENTENARIAN WOMAN...
content: Had the attendant dressed her in a blouse or tee with high polyester or rayon content, additional attention is required as its lower coefficient of friction would allow my layered efforts to slip into unacceptability. All the while hoping the model won't succumb to a sneeze or itch. And for these few wonderful minutes, everything is bliss. Another memory is born.

And had the day started with a chill, the attendant may have added a sweater to her ensemble. The extra thickness of fabric under my additional layering may look odd, so I'd unbutton the sweater and gently tug until it was secure off her shoulders.

images.description: Long sleeves are a bonus as they can be wrapped around the neck to appear as a matching scarf. But should there be a cuff and placket, ensure it doesn't make it into the photo frame.

And from my perch, blouses fall into two categories: the obvious and the not-so-obvious being front button units which requires laying it on her chest backwards with the shoulder seams lined up exactly where they'd otherwise be. The ever-present scarf hides the gaps and tames any wave in the fabric.

content: And as the photo series gathered steam, it became obvious the things that needed improvement.

The 'structured lapel'. It's a thing. Or could become a thing. Or maybe never become a thing. Anyway, I hear wrinkles can be the bane of many a woman. The dijon linen pants that were repurposed for this task turned out to be the structure behind all photos in the later series. With this homespun technology successfully tested and in place, some of the earlier style ideas were revamped for a completely different, and more polished, look.
Oh, one more thing on wrinkles. Press things. Remember an iron?

images.description: Defining a shoulder line is surprisingly important. Layering on a wide scarf over her shoulders may suggest a poncho if the camera frame is too wide .
Replicating the technique, but with a beautiful unstructured lightweight fabric, may suggest the classy grandmother of a linebacker.
And then there is surplus fabric clumped up by the wheelchair grip hinting at a 80s soap tv icon with pouffy sleeves.

content: Eventually, one binder clip was added to the tote bag and proved invaluable in tidying up things around the edge. For example, in the early days and trying to imply a cozy sweater, would have dumped a cozy sweater on moms lap and pulled its sleeves up and over her shoulders for the appearance of 'sweater' (IMG_0396.JPG). So with the sweater's cuffs now freely dangling out back, I had to either crop the shot or ensure her hairdo out back was bulky enough to distract from the numerous gaps. Later photos, of similar concept, would have the sweater dangling out back with the arms up and over the front for a more convincing appearance.

title: STAGING TECHNIQUES TO PHOTOGRAPH A FRAIL CENTENARIAN WOMAN...
content: The list of equipment to conjure together this impromptu studio is indeed concise and fixed (and humbling):  0.092 square metre (one square foot) of floor space from which the model would be centred ahead of the floral print, one west facing window, a pair of sheers on a rod, a pair of darkening drapes on a second rod, one LED ceiling fixture without dimming, a motorized hospital bed with guard rails, an armoire, a 3 drawer dresser with bookcase above, and a guest chair.

But there is a variable: the background. And it has the power to enhance the foreground by minimizing its own flaws. The floral background spans the photo array and is well known to family and friends. Dinner guests are particularly familiar as it hung in mom's dining room since time immemorial.

When the calendar rotates to a date that correlates to a special person or event in mom's experience, a familial photo or image would be added to the frame. Her photos that lead this essay was a perfect opportunity to synch her 99th birthday photos with her wedding photos seventy years earlier.

Others design elements can be quite subtle and may only become a bit more obvious over time. To get the print to fit in the bookcase unit, the top two shelves were removed and adjusting downward the pegs on the bottom shelf proved unsatisfactory as the lower gap appeared too frequently in the photos. Eventually removing it entirely permitted the print to sit lower. Problem solved.

The floral print has a reflective matte finish the poster shop applied to its surface. Tilting it in varying degrees along its vertical axis and/or along its horizontal axis gives a substantially different appearance within the photo array. That effect can then be exaggerated by repositioning the draperies for differential lighting. The best configuration is to draw the right sheer closed and leave the rest fully open.
Natural light is preferred and was the solitary source of illumination in all but two photos.
One subtle clue in identifying elements in the daily studio setup, is the reflection in her glasses.

My preference is to position mom's wheelchair a few degrees off straight on to the camera lens. While one wheelchair grip remains out of frame the other is behind her neck. Occasionally, a grip does appear because that particular photo was the best of the daily batch.

Loiter a bit ensuring any errant hairs are tucked out of sight. The model is patient and welcomes all the fussing. The stylist enjoys doing all the fussing. The photographer is happy to linger as the model succumbs to an occasional catnap. And when the model is ready for her closeup, a little fine tuning is all that is required.

title: PHOTOGRAPHING A WHEELCHAIR-BOUND CENTENARIAN WOMAN...
content: Most photos have her glancing outside the window. No wonder given that her room, and all that is in it, defines basic.

A few techniques evolved to redirect and refocus her gaze to the lens. Shutting a sheer may do the trick, shuffling her cup of coffee along the windowsill to hide behind a drape - and temporarily out of reach - may do the trick, perhaps a flipflop-free foot tapping her thigh may do the trick (otherwise known as a love tap), perhaps waiting out an impromptu doze may do the trick. Ultimately, her animated facial expression is usually a direct response to my animated expression or conversation.

Mom's concept of a camera is what dad would have had in the old days: a 35mm unit with an abundance of levers and dials. My handheld iPhone SE doesn't correlate with her, so after snapping a few photos, will the turn the phone around and show her the current batch of pics. She nods affirmatively.

Sitting at a distance and zooming in distorts her image, particularly if the lens is positioned low to avoid the wheelchair grips forever lurking in the background; sitting too close may allow the frame to include vinyl wallpaper beyond the confines of the floral print. I think I prefer sitting close - kneecap to kneecap - to the model and zooming in less giving her a trimmer, more natural, appearance.

title: CONVERSING WITH A CENTENARIAN WOMAN WITH MEMORY ISSUES...
content: What has made the most profound difference is communicating to her about things that would interest her. Even try things from the past. Keep names of family members in the conversation (even those who have gone ahead), chat about the weather or the changing seasons. Showing her the photo array of her own photos on my phone often generates warm recognition. I point to a photo, then point to her, and she likely responds affirmatively.

Viewing this array in its entirety has been somewhat of a revelation to me as it relates to my connection with my mom. While happy to visit each day, and continue the tradition of serving a freshly brewed cup of coffee, her natural facial expression reflects the natural reality of her parallel experience. And keeping in mind that living in a semi-private room within a long term care facility requires some adjustments.

Later photos have her engaged with the lens. To get there, I would chatter about the makeup and how it would go with the day's colour pick, chatter about the styling mousse and the technique for applying it. Seems that talented stylists and barbers have a natural gift of the gab. It can be kind of therapeutic, I guess.

The later photos have her peering into, perhaps even posing for, the lens. But to capture animation into her expression involved chatting with her about stuff she'd be interested in. I remember blue has been a favourite of hers. From a blue painted house which had blue wool carpet, to a blue Pontiac with blue velour seats, to a blue wool swing coat with trapunto. Lois remembers her as a fan of pink; perhaps from mom's bright pink picnic ware.

Blah. Blah. Blah. That word salad makes little sense. Let me read this section again. Blah. Blah. Blah. Geez. Come to think of it, is this an essay? Or a novella? Or a novelette? Well, to be sure, there is no fiction contained herein. There's likely a total word count that defines its category, and luckily, I know too few of them for this effort to inadvertently end up as a novel.

Anyway, I digress. Put another way: While seemingly content, or perhaps resolved, in her current circumstances, mom's core happiness can be gleaned by perusing her facial expressions: boredom, gradients of discomfort, and contentment are normal and span vast swaths of the photo array. But the lens would occasional capture a glimpse of glee or whimsey that, in itself, would elevate everything else to a whole new level. Which is all fine and good, but there just had to be more to it. And it came by means of things non-verbal: even a touch of the shoulder is meaningful. And the concept of a simple touch was eventually reciprocated. When I'd be fussing at her neckline trying to get a scarf to drape just so, she would lean her cheek to gently caress the back of my hand. Another memory is born.

title: AND THE ALL IMPORTANT SHOUT OUTS...
content: A shout out to mom's nursing team in particular and to the extended care community in general. You all have my highest respect. Thank you.
A shout out to my sister Lois and her daughter Melissa who, unknowingly, inspired me to sharpen the necessary skills. All with just a few kind words. Thank you.
A shout out to friends Jamie, Lance, Barb, Jeanette, Lisa, Chris, nurse Christine who furnished or loaned colours and/or bling. Thank you.
A shout out to a special Christmas Angel. This lovely person gifted mom with a bed jacket, a shawl and a blanket. Thank you.
A shout out to Warren, oracle of Omaha and his buddy Charlie. It works. Thank you.

title: AND WHAT ABOUT ME...
content: A long retired energy industry guy just trying to bring some sunshine to his mom.
Personally, I am drawn to the concept of Wu Wei (effortless action).
My presence in the universe of social media is scant.
And I possess a very dry sense of humour. Somewhat akin to skipping the vermouth.

title: AND FINALLY, SOME RANDOM THOUGHTS...
content: And here we are, a simple idea to keep our far flung family in the loop on mom and her journey. And with an unintended consequence of creating a portfolio of stylish photos for our family archives.
This read-only essay is not structured for reader comments. If you were inclined to comment, instead how about doing something nice for an elder today? And again tomorrow. Repeat.

'Catherine of Viceroy': feel free to share this search phrase amongst your friends and family who may enjoy the vignette. Over time, it will be interesting to see where serendipity takes this project.
